title: The {key:Ctrl-X} Sub-Completions
subtitle: Specialized completion: filenames, lines, dictionary, omni.
summary: Ctrl-X enters a sub-mode where the next key picks a completion source: Ctrl-F for filenames, Ctrl-L for whole lines, Ctrl-K for dictionary, Ctrl-O for omni-complete (language-aware), and more.

blocks.text: Plain {key:Ctrl-N} pulls from buffers. The {key:Ctrl-X} prefix opens a *sub-menu* where the next key chooses a more specific source.

blocks.title: How Omni-Complete Works
blocks.text:
{key:Ctrl-X}{key:Ctrl-O} calls the function set in the buffer-local `'omnifunc'` option. Filetype plugins set this for you: `pythoncomplete#Complete` for Python, `csscomplete#CompleteCSS` for CSS, `htmlcomplete#CompleteTags` for HTML, etc. The function is called twice — once to find the start column of the match, once to return matches.

This is the hook modern LSP plugins use to inject smart completions. If you've installed an LSP plugin like coc.nvim or vim-lsp, {key:Ctrl-X}{key:Ctrl-O} is what fires it.
{key:Ctrl-X}{key:Ctrl-F} is genuinely magical for typing paths — `./src/` then {key:Ctrl-X}{key:Ctrl-F} starts cycling the actual files in that directory.
